caveats: a: The occupation source is for NAICS 333300 rather than the six-digit industry.
a: The mapping measures task potential, not observed deployment, and product-level labor mixes vary substantially within the code.
rho: DOE examples establish technical feasibility across manufacturing rather than adoption rates in this industry.
rho: Legacy controls, fragmented data, product liability, and small-firm implementation capacity are not measured directly.
e: The assigned firm-count denominator is unavailable and was not replaced or re-estimated.
e: The Census definition confirms product breadth but does not measure recurring revenue or earnings-band eligibility.
s5: This is bounded judgment without observed six-digit transfer rates.
s5: The assigned firm-count denominator is missing, preventing a check against the implied absolute number of transfers.
q: No industry-wide evidence directly measures sharing of AI-enabled benefits.
q: Retention is likely higher in proprietary aftermarket service than in competitively bid equipment sales.
d5: The direct projection covers NAICS 333300, not this six-digit industry.
d5: Real output is not a pure physical-unit measure and the base period differs from the packet date.
d5: Office, photographic, laundry, vending, cooking, optical, and automotive-service equipment can follow materially different demand paths.
o: The estimate applies after the demand change in d5 and does not count the same volume loss twice.
o: Digital substitution risk is concentrated in only some of the many products covered by this NAICS code.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include laundries, food-service operators, retailers, offices, automotive service businesses, entertainment venues, and other commercial users. Revenue combines equipment sales with repeat model orders and, for some firms, aftermarket parts, upgrades, field service, software, and support tied to an installed base.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines materially different products and end markets, so the lens is narrowed to manufacturers with repeat equipment programs or installed-base aftermarket and service revenue. This is required for business-model coherence, not selected for attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off project manufacturers without recurring or repeat customer revenue, captive units, shells, financing vehicles, and manufacturers whose activity is principally outside this NAICS code.
lens.included_activities: US manufacturers in this code with repeat equipment programs, installed-base parts, upgrades, maintenance, diagnostics, or support sold to external commercial and service customers, where a transferable operating company can plausibly fall in the lower-middle-market band.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment by occupation for the broader machinery-manufacturing subsector NAICS 333300, not task exposure for NAICS 333310. I mapped management, business, engineering, office, sales, inspection, programming, and diagnostic work to partial generative-AI exposure while assigning little exposure to hands-on fabrication and assembly. Because the screened firms may have more service and support labor than the subsector, this proxy may understate exposure; because several included products remain production-intensive, it may also overstate it.
caveats: The occupation source is for NAICS 333300 rather than the six-digit industry.
The mapping measures task potential, not observed deployment, and product-level labor mixes vary substantially within the code.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from payroll and time-study data at a representative sample of NAICS 333310 manufacturers, split among production, engineering, sales, office, and aftermarket service roles, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The broader subsector's workforce is heavily physical: production occupations account for 41.26% of employment, including 17.72% in assembly and fabrication. AI can materially assist quoting, engineering documentation, scheduling, procurement, customer support, inspection, programming, and diagnostics, but it cannot directly replace much of machining, assembly, installation, or repair.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader NAICS 333300 to grow at 1.2% annually from 2024, while adjacent downstream service industries show different growth. I converted the broader annual output trend into a five-year quantity ratio and widened the interval for NAICS 333310's product and end-market mix. Real output is the closest published quantity proxy but can still reflect composition and quality changes; stronger downstream service activity could make this understate demand, while digital displacement of office and photographic machinery could make it overstate demand.
caveats: The direct projection covers NAICS 333300, not this six-digit industry.
Real output is not a pure physical-unit measure and the base period differs from the packet date.
Office, photographic, laundry, vending, cooking, optical, and automotive-service equipment can follow materially different demand paths.
d5.change_evidence: Product-level shipment volumes, order backlogs, installed-base replacement rates, and constant-quality forecasts for the principal NAICS 333310 product classes would materially tighten this ratio.
d5.rationale: The BLS broader-industry projection implies modest real-output growth. Physical-service end markets such as laundry, food service, automotive maintenance, vending, and amusement still require machinery, while office and photographic categories face secular digital substitution. The broad product mix warrants substantial downside and upside dispersion around the central projection.

caveats: The assigned firm-count denominator is unavailable and was not replaced or re-estimated.
The Census definition confirms product breadth but does not measure recurring revenue or earnings-band eligibility.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level business descriptions, revenue by equipment versus aftermarket, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA from a complete NAICS 333310 establishment census or private-company database would determine the eligible share.
e.rationale: A meaningful share of manufacturers should have repeat model sales or installed-base aftermarket revenue, but one-off machinery projects, product-only vendors, captive operations, and firms outside the target earnings band are excluded. Eligibility differs sharply across optical, laundry, vending, office, cooking, automotive-service, and other equipment segments.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 33331: labor compensation (L02) $8,935.9M / sectoral output (T30) $30,475.4M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $416,709 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 33331); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The estimate applies after the demand change in d5 and does not count the same volume loss twice.
Digital substitution risk is concentrated in only some of the many products covered by this NAICS code.
o.change_evidence: Product-class forecasts separating physical equipment purchases from software-only substitution, together with customer interviews on self-service procurement and maintenance, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five demand remains for accountable manufacturers and service organizations because customers need physical machines, safety, uptime, parts, installation, and warranty support. Software and self-service can reduce selected office, photographic, transaction, and diagnostic functions but do not eliminate the operator for most retained equipment demand.

caveats: No industry-wide evidence directly measures sharing of AI-enabled benefits.
Retention is likely higher in proprietary aftermarket service than in competitively bid equipment sales.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and renewal histories showing pricing formulas, discounting, service-level commitments, bid competition, and realized margins before and after productivity initiatives would materially change retention assumptions.
q.rationale: Manufacturers with differentiated products, proprietary service knowledge, and installed-base aftermarket relationships can retain part of productivity gains through margin expansion and improved service levels. Competitive equipment bids, distributor pressure, customer procurement, renewals, and transparent component costs will pass some benefit through.

caveats: DOE examples establish technical feasibility across manufacturing rather than adoption rates in this industry.
Legacy controls, fragmented data, product liability, and small-firm implementation capacity are not measured directly.
rho.change_evidence: A structured survey of NAICS 333310 plants documenting live AI use cases, deployment dates, realized labor hours, exception rates, and integration spending would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Implementation should be fastest in administrative, commercial, documentation, and support workflows. Engineering changes, automated visual inspection, predictive maintenance, and production optimization require validated data, equipment integration, cybersecurity controls, and accountable human review, limiting adoption within the horizon.

caveats: This is bounded judgment without observed six-digit transfer rates.
The assigned firm-count denominator is missing, preventing a check against the implied absolute number of transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, or BizBuySell matched to NAICS 333310, combined with owner-age and succession survey data from eligible firms, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Private industrial businesses face ordinary succession and consolidation pressures, but no industry-specific owner-age or completed-control-transfer series was found. Strategic sales, sponsor transactions, and family succession are plausible, while internal reorganizations and nontransferable closures do not qualify.

principal_driver: Installed-base parts, service, and technical support can pair durable physical demand with implementable AI gains in knowledge-heavy workflows.
principal_weakness: A highly heterogeneous product code and missing firm-denominator evidence make eligible-firm supply and demand durability difficult to generalize.

caveats: The a range is an authored mapping from the broader NAICS 333300 occupation mix rather than observed task exposure for six-digit NAICS 333310. S1 supports the cited labor composition, but the screened repeat-program and aftermarket lens may have a different role mix.

S2 establishes manufacturing AI use-case feasibility but does not measure adoption timing, realized labor substitution, integration constraints, or operator-required demand for NAICS 333310; rho and o remain appropriately labeled estimates.

S3 supports the 1.2% annual real-output projection for the broader BLS 333300 category, but the conversion to d5 and its application across the heterogeneous screened product mix remain a disclosed proxy rather than product-level quantity evidence.

S4 establishes product breadth and heterogeneity but does not measure the narrowed lens's eligible-firm share, value retention, or year-five operator requirement. The lens is coherently disclosed, yet its recurring-program and installed-base focus requires firm-level validation before conclusions can be generalized.
The missing n input prevents a complete base score, so the current STRESS_TEST_ONLY readiness and EVIDENCE_FIRST action appropriately dominate the estimate-heavy primitive ranges.

sources_audited.note: The cited BLS May 2023 NAICS 333300 occupation table was reopened through a reader fallback after the direct endpoint returned HTTP 403. It reports production occupations at 41.26% of industry employment and assemblers and fabricators at 17.72%, and it contains the broader management, business, engineering, office, sales, inspection, programming, installation, repair, and production mix described in the packet. It supports the disclosed occupation-mix proxy, not the authored task-exposure mapping or the numerical a range.

The DOE PDF was reopened directly. Section 3.3 describes AI-enabled generative product design, manufacturing automation, characterization, qualification, and operations optimization; sections 3.3.2 and 3.3.3 specifically discuss automated visual quality control, advanced characterization, connected IoT sensors, and predictive maintenance for factory capital equipment. This supports the packet's feasibility claims, while the packet honestly treats 333310 adoption, implementation, and operator-retention magnitudes as estimates.

The cited BLS Table 2.11 was reopened through a reader fallback after the direct endpoint returned HTTP 403. Its Commercial and service industry machinery manufacturing row, code 333300, reports real output of $23.5 billion in 2024, $26.4 billion in 2034, and a 1.2% compound annual output growth rate. That supports the stated broader projection and the disclosed five-year proxy transformation, not a direct product-level forecast for the screened lens.

The 2022 Census NAICS detail was reopened through a reader fallback after the direct endpoint returned HTTP 403. Its indexed activities include optical and photographic equipment, vending machines, commercial cooking equipment, non-household laundry machinery, office-type machinery, automotive-service equipment, amusement machinery, and related products. It supports the official scope and heterogeneity claim; the packet does not misrepresent it as direct evidence for recurring revenue, eligibility, retention, or operator-required demand.
